overview: Esper Self-Bounce operates as a tempo strategy that leverages return-to-hand effects to disrupt opponent plays while recycling your own permanents for repeated value. By bouncing key threats and lands, you maintain board presence, generate card advantage, and control the pace of the game. The deck aims to outmaneuver opponents through efficient sequencing and relentless pressure rather than raw power.
win_conditions: Direct damage using repeatedly cast creatures
Card advantage from bouncing and reactivating permanents
Resource denial by returning opponent lands and threats to hand

keep_criteria: Hands with early bounce spells and 1-drops
Two-mana spells that enable turn-two disruption
Draw spells to stabilize in longer games
Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including basics for Boomerang Basics and WUB mana), at least one bounce enabler (Stormchaser's Talent or Kaito), and a turn-1 or turn-2 play.
mulligan_criteria: Hands with three or more lands
Decks devoid of bounce effects or recursion
Mulligan on 3+ lands with no bounce spells, 1-2 lands with only bounce spells and no early threats, or hands lacking a way to protect your engine.

early_game: Play bounce spells to disrupt early threats
Establish land drops while holding interaction
Use low-cost creatures to apply pressure
if you have a cheap bounce spell and a Boomerang Basic, consider bouncing it for early mana or card advantage.
Turn 2: Deploy Kaito or a bounce enabler, bounce a Boomerang Basic or itself to trigger ETB effects, and establish a resilient board.
Sequence lands to ensure WUB availability for interaction on opponent's turn.
mid_game: Recycle your own permanents to generate value
Target opponent's key pieces with return-to-hand effects
Maintain card advantage through draw synergies
Hoarded bounce spells for key ETB triggers or to protect Kaito.
Use Boomerang Basics for flexible mana acceleration and card advantage.
prioritize triggers that generate tempo or card advantage.
Monitor your graveyard to ensure bounce doesn't inadvertently feed opponent recursion.
Commit threats only after securing bounce protection or having interaction ready.
late_game: Lock opponent out by repeatedly bouncing lands and threats
Use accumulated resources to close out the game
Prioritize bouncing high-impact permanents over low-value ones
Kaito, Bane of Nightmares as a protected value engine or combat finisher, repeated Boomerang Basic bounces for lethal mana/card advantage, and Stormchaser's Talent for late-game recursion.
Win by overwhelming with triggered value or closing out with a protected threat that cannot be easily removed.
Go all-in when you have bounce protection, enough mana to survive a turn, and the opponent has no clear answer.
Commit when you can trigger multiple ETB effects in response to their removal or when you can close the game before they can stabilize.

key_cards.name: Kaito, Bane of Nightmares
key_cards.notes: Use its abilities to trigger bounce synergies and punish opponent's board state. Keep it protected until you can leverage its full potential.

key_cards.name: Stormchaser's Talent
key_cards.notes: Cast to refill your hand after bouncing permanents, ensuring you always have answers or follow-up threats.

key_cards.name: Boomerang Basics
key_cards.notes: Deploy these spells to return opponent threats to hand while bouncing your own lands or creatures for repeated activations.

sequencing_tips: Hold bounce spells in hand to respond to opponent's turns
Bounce your own permanents only when you have a clear follow-up
Prioritize bouncing opponent's lands when they are ahead on resources
Use draw spells before committing to a bounce sequence to ensure hand size
Common mistake: Bouncing permanents without calculating ETB triggers or mana needs.
Always ask what value you gain from each bounce.
Unique interaction: Boomerang Basics can be bounced for mana/card advantage even when tapped, enabling surprise turns.
Kaito's abilities likely synergize with exile/bounce

matchups.advice: Focus on bouncing early threats and lands to stall until you can stabilize. Use your creatures to apply pressure while holding interaction.
Bounce their key counterspells and lands to disrupt their game plan. Use your recursion to outvalue them over time.
Disrupt their curve by bouncing their threats and lands. Maintain board presence to force them to use resources inefficiently.

Use bounce spells as free spells since they resolve or bounce on resolution. Cast threats at end step, use Kaito's abilities to protect your spells, or bounce their counterspell if possible. Prioritize spells with flash or instant-speed triggers to bypass counters.
Prioritize early interaction and spot removal. Use bounce to delay their mana rocks and disrupt their acceleration. Bring in board wipes and artifact hate to slow their game plan while you stabilize.

Win by having better card advantage and more efficient bounce spells. Prioritize targeting their Kaito or key bounce enablers. Use counterspells to disrupt their recursion loops and force them to replay their engine at a disadvantage.

common_threats: Watch for opponent mana development (ramp vs control), graveyard activity (recursion threats), and board state (when they assemble a win condition).
Key tells: Holding up mana on opponent's turn suggests counterspells or instant-speed hate
playing multiple permanents suggests they're going wide or setting up a combo.
